comments.author: @NANA1TO9GK
comments.text:
2. Ezekiel’s Vision of Renewal
The most famous prophetic vision about the Dead Sea comes from Ezekiel 47:8–12. The prophet sees a river flowing from Jerusalem, flowing eastward into the Arabah and emptying into the Dead Sea. The salty waters are transformed into fresh water, bringing life to the land:

“When it empties into the sea, the salty water there becomes fresh” (Ezekiel 47:8).

“Wherever the river flows, there will be swarms of living creatures and a great number of fish” (Ezekiel 47:9).

Fruit trees grow along the riverbanks, bearing fruit every month and producing healing leaves....Modern Fulfillment
In recent decades, actual freshwater rivers have been observed flowing into the Dead Sea, nourishing plant life and even supporting fish populations—something once thought impossible. This has been seen as a partial fulfillment of Ezekiel’s prophecy, showing the reality of God’s restorative work in the world

comments.text: What is a common era?    Don't you mean Before Christ?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Same dates, just different labelling — 363 CE is the same year as 363 AD. "Common era" is the standard used in most modern archaeology and academic scholarship, which is the tradition we follow on this channel. Both systems count from the same point.

comments.author: @PictorialJourney
comments.text: I never realized how much history was carved into the landscape of Jordan until watching this. The way you explained the Nabataeans’ engineering—specifically those water channels in the Siq—was fascinating; it’s incredible that they found such precise angles by trial and error thousands of years ago. It really puts the country's resilience into perspective, seeing how they’ve balanced that ancient heritage with the modern challenges of being in such a difficult neighborhood. The whole documentary felt like a thoughtful, quiet journey. Thank you for sharing such a deep look at a place that clearly has so many hidden layers. 🏜✨🙏

replies.author: @WorldDiscovered_Official
replies.text: Egypt, Jordan, Iraq, Iran, Turkey, Syria, Lebanon, Yemen, and Ethiopia. I have visited a few of these countries, and they are truly beautiful, from their history and culture to their people. They are places worth exploring. Although today, with industrialization, these countries are often considered cradles of human civilization and have changed a great deal, their ancient soul and cultural depth can still be felt everywhere.

replies.text: That's a fair observation... Jordan's stability is genuinely unusual for the region. A lot of it comes down to the Hashemite dynasty's approach to diplomacy and absorbing pressure rather than projecting it. It's a fascinating case study regardless of where you land on the bigger question.
